message.content:
Nice. Since you have two collections (movies and users), a good next move is to explore the data a bit more to understand its shape and contents. Here are practical options—pick what matches your goal, and I can walk you through it or run it if you want.

Recommended quick start
- Peek a few documents from each collection:
  - Movies: db.movies.find({}).limit(5)
  - Users: db.users.find({}).limit(5)

Other useful next steps
- Get document counts:
  - Movies: db.movies.countDocuments({})
  - Users: db.users.countDocuments({})

- Inspect indexes (to see what’s optimized):
  - Movies: db.movies.getIndexes()
  - Users: db.users.getIndexes()

- Create helpful indexes (example):
  - Movies: db.movies.createIndex({ year: 1 })
  - Users: db.users.createIndex({ email: 1 }, { unique: true })

- Simple aggregations to summarize data:
  - Movies by year: db.movies.aggregate([{ $group: { _id: "$year", count: { $sum: 1 } } }, { $sort: { _id: 1 } }])
  - Users by country: db.users.aggregate([{ $group: { _id: "$country", count: { $sum: 1 } } }, { $sort: { _id: 1 } }])

- Add or modify data:
  - Insert a movie: db.movies.insertOne({ title: "New Movie", year: 2024, genre: "Drama" })
  - Update a user: db.users.updateOne({ _id: ObjectId("...") }, { $set: { lastLogin: new Date() } })

- Data export/import (backup or migration):
  - Export a collection: mongoexport --db mydb --collection movies --out movies.json

- Validate or enforce schema basics (MongoDB schema validation is optional but helpful for consistency):
  - Examples: require certain fields, set types, or enforce unique emails on users

Tell me which option sounds best, or describe your specific goal (e.g., “I want to find all movies from 2015 in genre X” or “I want to know how many users signed up per month”), and I’ll provide step-by-step commands or guide you through it. If you’d like, I can also suggest a concrete next action based on your objective.
